Her statements, stacked on top of the surveillance and a growing pile of evidence, basically confirmed what investigators already suspected. The Taos managed to move between 42 and a half and 45 and a half ounces into La Crosse County from August 2023 through January 2024. She also stated that she believed her husband had been receiving approximately 2 ounces of meth per month from January through August.

In the six months leading up to his arrest, Tudang was involved in distributing or possessing with the intent to distribute more than 5 and a half kilograms of methamphetamine. According to his own statements, he sourced meth from various suppliers located in Minnesota, California, Canada, and Mexico and acted as the main supplier for at least six other drug distributors. Throughout the investigation, a series of controlled buys were conducted, during which Tudang and Ju unknowingly screwed themselves over right in the ass and sold a total of 203.9 grams of methamphetamine to a confidential informant in La Crosse. In at least one of these transactions, Tudang was armed, and he later attempted to obtain additional firearms, reportedly to provide to his drug sources. The couple took a break in December 2023, but resumed activity in early January, and on the day of their arrest, Ju said they traveled to Minneapolis to purchase 1 pound from a man known only as Joe, who she described as Hispanic.

In the end, both Tudang and Ju were charged with multiple felony offenses, possession with intent to deliver methamphetamine and maintaining a drug trafficking place. Tudang was released on a $7,500 signature bond. Ju on a $5,000 bond. But given the volume of drugs involved and Tudang's suspected connections, the state referred the case up to federal prosecutors. When a US district judge finally sentenced him, he described Tudang as a serious risk to the community, and on March 3rd, 2025, Tudang and Ju pleaded guilty to federal drug charges. Ultimately, Tudang was sentenced to 90 months in prison, seven and a half years, and Ju was sentenced to five years of probation. Some believe she was forced into Tudang's operation out of fear, while others wonder if she was just as involved and just better at hiding it. This case was a reminder that drug trafficking doesn't always come with a warning label. Sometimes, it's not the shady figure in a dark alley. It's an ex-couple driving home, looking like they had just finished a weekend antique shopping trip, or it's a quiet man in his 50s, living what seems like an average life, who turns out to be running a side hustle as a full-blown drug lord.
